original_abstract:
This paper describes the results of the ParlaMint II project, which has significantly expanded and enhanced a collection of comparable corpora of European parliamentary debates. The dataset now encompasses proceedings from 29 countries and autonomous regions, totaling over one billion words and covering the period from 2015 to at least 2022. The corpora are uniformly encoded using the Text Encoding Initiative (TEI) guidelines and include detailed metadata for approximately 24,000 speakers. Linguistic annotations are provided across multiple levels, including Universal Dependencies syntax and named entity recognition.

Beyond data expansion, the project introduced several qualitative improvements: the localization of metadata into national languages, the inclusion of new variables such as the political orientation of parties, and the machine translation of the entire collection into English to facilitate cross-linguistic research. Furthermore, the translated versions have been enriched with semantic tagging. The paper details the collaborative compilation workflow, the infrastructure for validation and distribution via CLARIN services, and the development of pilot spoken corpora for selected languages. Finally, we provide a quantitative overview of the resource and discuss its applications in the social sciences and humanities.
humanized_abstract:
This paper presents the outcome of the ParlaMint II project, which enlarged and enriched the set of comparable corpora of European national parliaments’ proceedings. The resulting resource contains the records of speeches delivered by the representatives of 29 European countries and their respective regions, containing over a billion words and spanning from 2015 up to at least 2022. The language resources are processed into the Text Encoding Initiative (TEI) format and are accompanied by metadata for every single one of the 24,000 identified speakers. Furthermore, the speeches are enriched with a variety of linguistic annotations ranging from Universal Dependencies syntax to named entity recognition.

We added new features rather than simply increasing the amount of data available to users. Specifically, we located all metadata in national languages, added new variables such as the political stance of parties and made all documents machine translated to English to support cross-language research. The translations were also enriched with semantic tagging. In this paper we describe the collaboration and workflow behind the compilation, validation and distribution of the resources via the CLARIN infrastructure as well as the development of pilot spoken corpora for each language. We give also an overview of the characterisation of the resource and highlight some of the applications of the resource within the social sciences and humanities.

The language resources are processed into the Text Encoding Initiative (TEI) format and are accompanied by metadata for every single one of the 24,000 identified speakers. Furthermore, the speeches are enriched with a variety of linguistic annotations ranging from Universal Dependencies syntax to named entity recognition.

We added new features rather than simply increasing the amount of data available to users. Specifically, we located all metadata in national languages, added new variables such as the political stance of parties and made all documents machine translated to English to support cross-language research. The translations were also enriched with semantic tagging. We give also an overview of the characterisation of the resource and highlight some of the applications of the resource within the social sciences and humanities.
document.input:
This paper describes the results of the ParlaMint II project, which has significantly expanded and enhanced a collection of comparable corpora of European parliamentary debates. The dataset now encompasses proceedings from 29 countries and autonomous regions, totaling over one billion words and covering the period from 2015 to at least 2022. The corpora are uniformly encoded using the Text Encoding Initiative (TEI) guidelines and include detailed metadata for approximately 24,000 speakers. Linguistic annotations are provided across multiple levels, including Universal Dependencies syntax and named entity recognition.

Beyond data expansion, the project introduced several qualitative improvements: the localization of metadata into national languages, the inclusion of new variables such as the political orientation of parties, and the machine translation of the entire collection into English to facilitate cross-linguistic research. Furthermore, the translated versions have been enriched with semantic tagging. The paper details the collaborative compilation workflow, the infrastructure for validation and distribution via CLARIN services, and the development of pilot spoken corpora for selected languages. Finally, we provide a quantitative overview of the resource and discuss its applications in the social sciences and humanities.
